title: WEF Chief Economists' Outlook May 2026: Hormuz Shock and Stagflation Risk
stale_items: Survey fieldwork ran 6-17 April 2026, so conflict-duration scenarios (Hormuz reopening in April/May/June) and market snapshots (VIX 17.08 on 7 May, Brent above $110 'at time of writing') may be overtaken by events as of July 2026
executive_summary: Sentiment has deteriorated sharply: 89% of 38 surveyed chief economists expect global growth to weaken over the next 12 months (21% significantly weaker), erasing January 2026 optimism after US/Israel air strikes on Iran (28 Feb 2026) and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz — previously ~10% of global seaborne trade and ~20 million bpd of oil, now reduced to a trickle; a 7 April two-week ceasefire was followed by a 12 April US naval blockade, with Brent briefly above $125/bbl and above $110 at time of writing.

Inflation is the dominant concern: 94% expect global inflation to rise (IMF sees 4.1% in 2025 rising to 4.4% in 2026), driven by energy and food; yet 58% still do not expect a global recession within 12 months, and 6 in 10 do not expect the global economy to become more resilient either. Only 53% now expect the US dollar to weaken, down from 78% in September 2025.

Debt-market stress is building: 79% expect private debt market volatility to rise and 74% public debt volatility; private credit loan markdowns began in March and a new CDS market against flagship private credit funds has emerged; government/corporate borrowing hit a record in 2025 and sovereign interest spending has climbed from 2% to nearly 3% of GDP in four years. 68% expect higher equity volatility (VIX 17.08 on 7 May vs >30 in late March).

AI sentiment is cooling: 92% still expect adoption to increase, but median expected timelines to meaningful productivity gains lengthened across nearly all industries except IT (<1 year) and education; financial/professional/real estate services ~1 year, defence within 2 years (US budget pivoting to autonomous systems, $54bn Pentagon AI request).

Crisis severity benchmarking (Figure 8, COVID-19=100): the current Hormuz closure already exceeds the 2025 tariff turmoil (55 vs 40 if reopened in May); closure into H2 2026 scores 83.5 — approaching pandemic-scale impact. Energy & materials is the most disrupted industry (97% high/very high), then supply chain/transport (76%, ~2,000 vessels/~5% of global tonnage stranded in the Gulf), leisure & travel (65%), defence (64%); UAE decided to leave OPEC; Qatar's Ras Laffan strike (18 March) removed ~1/5 of global LNG supply with 3-5 year repairs.

Price transmission is regionally uneven: 62% expect significant energy price increases in South-East Asia vs only 13% in the US and 11% in China (US now a net crude exporter); food inflation risk is near-universal — 97% expect Indian food prices to rise (33% significantly) — with ~30% of internationally traded fertilizer normally transiting Hormuz and sulphur/ammonia shipments already broken (FAO index +2.4% in March 2026 vs +12.6% in March 2022).

Regional divergence: US moderate growth (74%) but 61% expect weak hiring and Fed on hold; China improved (77% expect moderate+ growth, Q1 +5% y/y, deflation ending, 85% see unchanged policy rates, 68% looser fiscal); Europe deteriorated (65% weak/very weak, ECB cut 2026 euro-area GDP 0.3pp to 0.9%, 61% expect ECB tightening — stagflation risk); India strongest (52% strong/very strong growth, ~6.5% GDP FY2026-27, but 61% expect high inflation, RBI held repo at 5.25% and burned $40bn of FX reserves in March defending the rupee); Japan weakest DM (77% weak, 0.8% growth); MENA collapsed (88% weak/very weak, World Bank growth ex-Iran 4.2%→1.6%, tourism losing $600mn/day); Sub-Saharan Africa steady at 4.1% but 67% expect high inflation (vs 25% prior).

Multinational business-environment ranking: US 65%, India 56%, South-East Asia 50%, Europe 44%, China 35% (intense competition, slim margins), Japan/LatAm 21%, MENA 0% — a stark barometer of geopolitical-risk repricing; India credited for open trade policy including the new EU-India trade agreement (January 2026). Global trade hit a record $35tn (+7.5%) in 2025 and FDI rose 14% to $1.6tn, but the 2026 investment outlook is 'all but certain' to be weak.

key_takeaways.takeaway: A synchronised stagflationary supply shock (89% see weaker growth, 94% see higher inflation) compresses corporate margins and raises funding costs simultaneously — negative for credit quality across energy-importing economies, with limited central-bank room to ease.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Stagflation is the worst regime for leveraged issuers: revenue slows while input costs and policy rates stay elevated. Track Brent crude vs the ~$110-125/bbl range and IMF global inflation (4.4% 2026 forecast) as the master variables for downgrade momentum in energy-intensive sectors.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Private credit is flagged as the epicentre of the next funding stress: loan markdowns began in March 2026 and a CDS market against flagship private credit funds has emerged, while 79% of chief economists expect private debt volatility to rise.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: CDS creation on private credit funds means stress is now being priced and shorted — a classic late-cycle signal (echoes of 2007 ABX). For Indian credit work, watch spillover to offshore private-credit funding of Indian corporates/real estate and AIF credit funds; track private credit fund NAV markdowns and CDS spreads quarterly.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Sovereign debt dynamics are deteriorating globally — record borrowing in 2025 set to rise again in 2026, interest spending up from 2% to ~3% of GDP in four years, long-duration demand weakening and maturities shortening — pressuring term premia and crowding out corporate issuance.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Rising sovereign supply plus 74% expecting public debt volatility means wider and more volatile benchmark yields that reprice all corporate credit. Track 10-year G-sec/UST term premium and average sovereign maturity profiles; shortening maturities raise rollover risk at the sovereign level that cascades to bank/NBFC funding costs.

key_takeaways.takeaway: India is the relative credit safe haven — strongest growth expectations in the survey (52% strong/very strong, ~6.5% GDP for FY2026-27) and second most attractive business destination (56%) — but external and inflation vulnerabilities are live: RBI shed $40bn of FX reserves in March to defend the rupee and 61% expect high/very high Indian inflation.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Growth supports Indian issuer fundamentals, but a $40bn one-month reserve drawdown is a material external-stress signal; imported energy/food inflation (CPI 3.4% in March, yet to reflect the full shock) could force RBI off its 5.25% neutral hold (25% already expect tightening). Track monthly RBI FX reserves and CPI food/fuel components — a reserve slide below ~$600bn plus CPI above 5% flips the India story from positive to pressured.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The fertilizer-food transmission channel is the sleeper risk for Indian rural credit: ~30% of internationally traded fertilizer transits Hormuz, sulphur/ammonia shipments have broken down, and 97% of respondents expect Indian food prices to rise (33% significantly) — hitting farm input costs and rural cash flows with a lag into late 2026 harvests.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Rural/agri stress arrives with a 2-3 quarter lag after fertilizer supply shocks, threatening microfinance, tractor/agri-equipment finance and rural NBFC asset quality just as funding costs rise. Track domestic DAP/urea prices and FAO Food Price Index monthly; a repeat of the 2022-style spike (+12.6% in one month) would be the trigger to cut exposure to unsecured rural lenders.

key_takeaways.takeaway: MENA's collapse (88% expect weak/very weak growth; World Bank cut growth ex-Iran from 4.2% to 1.6%; zero votes as an attractive business environment) endangers the GCC remittance engine — migrant workers are 76-95% of GCC private-sector employment — a direct channel into South Asian external accounts and deposit franchises.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: India is the world's largest remittance recipient and Kerala/Gulf-corridor deposits fund several Indian banks; GCC labour-market disruption plus Saudi Arabia's widening deficit threaten this flow. Track RBI monthly remittance/NRI deposit data — relevant to FCNR-B mobilisation economics and small private banks with Gulf-heavy deposit bases.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Policy divergence creates FX and carry risk: 61% expect ECB tightening while 65% expect looser euro-area fiscal; the Fed is split three ways; China holds rates (85%) with record fiscal expansion (68% looser); and dollar conviction has cracked (53% expect weakening vs 78% in Sep 2025) — volatile cross-currency funding conditions for EM borrowers.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Simultaneous fiscal loosening and monetary tightening in Europe is the classic recipe for bond-market accidents (Italy already lobbying to relax EU fiscal rules for energy). For Indian issuers with ECB/FCY borrowings, hedging costs will swing; track EUR and USD swap spreads and the dollar index as leading indicators of external commercial borrowing cost resets.

key_takeaways.takeaway: China's improvement (Q1 +5% y/y, PPI positive for the first time in 41 months, deflation ending) is a mixed blessing: it stabilises global demand but a 'growing inflow of cheaper Chinese goods' is tempering South-East Asia's outlook — import-competition risk for manufacturers across Asia including India.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: End of Chinese producer deflation removes one global disinflationary anchor while Chinese export redirection pressures margins of Indian steel, chemicals and consumer-durable makers. Track China PPI and India's trade deficit with China; widening deficits would justify anti-dumping actions and margin-based downgrades in metals/chemicals.

key_takeaways.takeaway: AI productivity payback is being pushed out across most industries (except IT at <1 year and education), meaning debt-funded AI capex — a key pillar still carrying US growth — may see returns arrive later than leverage assumptions imply.
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Longer adoption cycles concentrated in large firms mean the 'AI premium' in tech/data-centre credit could deflate; supply constraints (power, chips, skills) are already emerging. Track data-centre capex versus incremental revenue for hyperscaler-adjacent issuers, and Indian IT services deal wins as an early read on whether the ~1-year gains in financial/professional services materialise.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Aviation and travel credit is under acute pressure: 65% see high/very high disruption, Gulf hub departures collapsed, Middle East tourism is contracting, and ballooning jet fuel prices could render many routes uneconomic (Lufthansa already cutting 20,000 flights).
key_takeaways.analyst_lens: Fuel is 25-40% of airline cost structures; a sustained Brent >$110 plus rerouting around the Gulf compresses margins on India-Gulf and Europe-Asia corridors. Track jet fuel crack spreads and load factors on Gulf routes for Indian carriers and airport concession credits.

entities_impacted.entity: Reserve Bank of India (central bank of India)
entities_impacted.impact: Held repo at 5.25% in April 2026 with a neutral stance but spent $40bn of FX reserves in March defending the rupee; 61% of surveyed economists expect high Indian inflation and 25% expect tightening — squeezed between currency defence and growth support, shaping bank/NBFC funding costs.

Strongest growth expectations in the survey (~6.5% GDP, 52% strong/very strong) and #2 business destination (56%), but exposed via energy imports (41% expect significant energy price rises), near-universal food-price inflation expectations (97%), rupee depreciation pressure and GCC remittance risk.

Kept rates unchanged in April citing elevated inflation, low job gains and rising uncertainty; survey split three ways on next move (32% looser/37% unchanged/32% tighter) — anchor for global dollar funding conditions.

Cut its 2026 euro-area GDP forecast 0.3pp to 0.9%; 61% expect tightening into an energy-shock-driven inflation jump (2.6% in March) while 65% expect looser fiscal — stagflation-management risk for European bank and sovereign credit.

entities_impacted.entity: Private credit groups / private credit funds
entities_impacted.impact: Loan portfolios marked down since March 2026 and a new CDS market against flagship private credit funds has emerged; 79% expect rising private debt volatility — refinancing and mark-to-market risk for leveraged borrowers dependent on this channel.

Most disrupted industry (97% high/very high): Hormuz closure cut oil flows from ~20mn bpd to a trickle and removed ~1/5 of global LNG supply (Ras Laffan, 3-5 year repairs); price surges generate windfall profits for producers but severe input-cost shocks for importers; UAE exit from OPEC adds market-structure uncertainty.

65% see high/very high disruption: Gulf hub departures collapsed, Middle East tourism losing ~$600mn/day, rerouting and jet-fuel inflation making routes uneconomic — negative for airline and airport credits globally.

entities_impacted.entity: Agriculture, forestry and fishing / fertilizer supply chain
entities_impacted.impact: ~30% of internationally traded fertilizer transits Hormuz; sulphur/ammonia shipments broke down simultaneously, threatening future harvests and rural incomes — lagged asset-quality risk for agri lenders and food-price risk for import-dependent countries.

entities_impacted.entity: Middle East and North Africa / GCC economies
entities_impacted.impact: Sharpest deterioration: 88% expect weak/very weak growth, World Bank cut ex-Iran growth to 1.6%, Saudi deficit widening, zero votes as attractive business environment; disruption to GCC labour markets (76-95% migrant private employment) threatens remittances to South Asia.

Outlook improved (77% expect moderate+ growth, Q1 +5%, PPI positive after 41 months of deflation, record fiscal push) but export redirection of cheap goods pressures competing manufacturers across Asia; relatively shielded from the fuel crunch (only 11% expect significant energy price rises).

Bears the brunt of the energy shock (62% expect significant price increases; Philippines declared an energy emergency and hiked rates) with 80-100% import dependence on wheat/soybeans/rice; still ranked #3 business destination (50%) on supply-chain diversification flows.

learning: Benchmark geopolitical shocks quantitatively: the survey's severity index (COVID-19=100) shows a Hormuz closure into H2 2026 scoring 83.5 vs 40 for the 2025 tariff turmoil — a usable scaling device for portfolio stress-test calibration instead of binary war/no-war scenarios.

Energy shocks hit credit in sequenced waves — fuel first, then fertilizer, then food, then rural incomes — so agri/rural lender asset-quality deterioration should be modelled with a 2-3 quarter lag from the commodity spike, not coincident with it.

A single-month FX reserve drawdown ($40bn by RBI in March 2026) can be a sharper early-warning indicator of external stress than growth or ratings optics; monitor reserve adequacy alongside headline GDP when assessing EM sovereign-linked credit.

The emergence of a CDS market referencing private credit funds is itself a credit signal: when instruments are created to short an asset class, price discovery of hidden stress follows — watch derivative-market innovation as a leading indicator of where the next credit cycle breaks.
